figures.fig-7-2: This is a schematic anatomical illustration presenting the hierarchical organization of skeletal muscle fiber (myocyte) structure across three levels of magnification: (a) myogenesis via myoblast fusion, (b) fasciculus organization, and (c) sarcomere ultrastructure. Panel (a) depicts the developmental fusion of myoblasts into an immature muscle fiber, with satellite cells remaining adjacent to the formed fiber. Panel (b) shows the organization of a fasciculus, labeling the satellite cell positioned between the perimysium around fascicle and the muscle fiber surface, the sarcolemma (plasma membrane), sarcoplasm (cytoplasm), myofibrils (contractile units), mitochondria, endomysium (connective tissue surrounding individual fibers), and multiple peripherally located nuclei characteristic of multinucleated syncytial muscle cells. Panel (c) provides detailed ultrastructure of a single muscle fiber in cross-section and longitudinal view, identifying the sarcoplasmic reticulum (specialized endoplasmic reticulum for calcium storage), sarcolemma, sarcoplasm, sarcolemma, mitochondria positioned between myofibrils, the triad structure formed by transverse tubules (T-tubules) flanked by terminal cisterns of the sarcoplasmic reticulum, and the sarcomere with its thick filaments (myosin), thin filaments (actin), and Z-disc boundaries that define each sarcomere unit. This illustration directly supports the surrounding text describing muscle fiber composition (75% water, 20% protein), the sarcolemma definition as plasma membrane plus basal lamina, and the critical role of T-tubules as membranous channels extending perpendicular from the sarcolemma into the fiber interior for excitation-contraction coupling. The figure comprehensively maps all structural components from cellular (satellite cells, nuclei) to subcellular (myofibrils, sarcoplasmic reticulum) to molecular (thick/thin filaments) levels, enabling understanding of how muscle contraction occurs within the hierarchical architecture of skeletal muscle tissue.
figures.fig-7-8: This is a schematic process diagram illustrating the nine sequential steps of neuromuscular signal transmission and excitation–contraction coupling in skeletal muscle. Step 1 shows a nerve impulse arriving at the axon terminal of a motor neuron, triggering acetylcholine (ACh) release from synaptic vesicles into the synaptic cleft. Step 2 depicts ACh diffusing across the synaptic cleft, binding to ACh receptors on the motor end plate, and triggering a muscle action potential (AP), while Step 3 shows acetylcholinesterase in the synaptic cleft degrading ACh to prevent continuous stimulation—directly illustrating the surrounding text's description of ACh termination by AChE attached to extracellular matrix collagen fibers. Step 4 traces the muscle AP propagating along the transverse tubule (T-tubule) into the muscle fiber interior, where it opens Ca²⁺ release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) membrane, allowing calcium ions (depicted as purple circles) to flood from the SR (yellow organelle with ~10 mM Ca²⁺ concentration, matching the text's stated millimolar storage level) into the sarcoplasm. Step 5 shows Ca²⁺ binding to troponin on the thin (actin) filament, exposing myosin-binding sites previously blocked by the troponin-tropomyosin complex. Step 6 illustrates contraction via ATP-powered myosin head binding, swiveling, and release (power strokes), pulling thin filaments toward the sarcomere center and shortening the muscle. Steps 7–8 depict relaxation: Ca²⁺ release channels in the SR close and Ca²⁺-active transport pumps (using ATP) restore low sarcoplasmic Ca²⁺ levels by pumping ions back into the SR, allowing the troponin-tropomyosin complex to slide back and re-block myosin-binding sites on actin, culminating in Step 9 muscle relaxation. The diagram directly visualizes the EC coupling process described in section 2.2.2, emphasizing Ca²⁺ release from the SR as the integral link between electrical excitation (AP propagation) and mechanical contraction (force generation).
figures.fig-7-6: This is a multi-panel anatomical illustration comprising schematic diagrams (a–c) and a scanning electron micrograph (d) depicting the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) and the molecular mechanism of acetylcholine-mediated neuromuscular transmission. Panel (a) shows the macroscopic architecture of the NMJ, labeling the axon collateral of the somatic motor neuron, axon terminal, synaptic end bulb, the neuromuscular junction itself, sarcolemma (muscle fiber membrane), and myofibril bundles within the muscle fiber. Panel (b) provides an enlarged view of the junction region, highlighting the axon terminal, direction of nerve impulse propagation, synaptic vesicles containing acetylcholine (ACh), the synaptic end bulb, motor end plate on the sarcolemma, and the synaptic cleft (space) separating the presynaptic and postsynaptic membranes. Panel (c) illustrates the four-step sequence of synaptic transmission: (1) ACh is released from synaptic vesicles in the synaptic end bulb, (2) ACh binds to ACh receptors (transmembrane proteins) embedded in the motor end plate, (3) this binding triggers a muscle action potential (indicated by sodium ions Na⁺ entering through ion channels in the junctional folds), and (4) ACh is broken down by acetylcholinesterase to terminate the signal. Panel (d) is a scanning electron micrograph (SEM, 1650× magnification) showing the three-dimensional relationship between the axon terminal, multiple synaptic end bulbs, axon collateral branches, skeletal muscle fibers, and a nearby blood capillary. This figure directly supports the surrounding text's explanation that the motor end plate is the specialized region of the sarcolemma containing ACh receptors, which facilitate cell-to-cell communication by receiving ACh diffusing across the synaptic cleft from the presynaptic nerve terminal, thereby initiating the signal for muscle fiber contraction.
figures.fig-7-3: This figure is a composite schematic diagram and electron micrograph illustrating the ultrastructure of the sarcomere, the fundamental contractile unit of skeletal muscle myofibrils. Panel (a) presents a three-dimensional cylindrical representation of a myofibril segment, clearly labeling the Z-disc (Z-線) boundaries that demarcate each sarcomere (typically 2–2.5 μm in length), the central M-line (M-線) that bisects the sarcomere, thick filaments composed primarily of myosin protein, and thin filaments composed primarily of actin protein, with the striated banding pattern visible along the length. Panel (b) provides a detailed longitudinal schematic of two adjacent sarcomeres, explicitly delineating the I-band (I-帶, light band containing predominantly thin actin filaments), the A-band (A-帶, dark band containing thick myosin filaments with zones of actin–myosin overlap), the H-zone (H-區) in the center of the A-band where only thick filaments are present, the M-line at the midpoint of the H-zone, the Z-discs anchoring the thin filaments at sarcomere boundaries, and the elastic titin filaments extending from the Z-disc to the M-line to maintain structural integrity. Panel (c) is an electron micrograph of longitudinally sectioned muscle tissue showing the repeating pattern of Z-discs, A-bands, I-bands, H-zones, and sarcomeres as dark and light striations, with surrounding cellular organelles including mitochondria, sarcoplasmic reticulum (肌漿網), transverse tubules (橫小管, T-tubules), terminal cisternae (終池), and the sarcolemma (肌膜) visible in the intercellular space. This figure directly supports the textual explanation that the I-band contains actin (remembering "lIght"), the A-band contains myosin (remembering "dArk"), and that the zone of overlap where thick and thin filaments interdigitate is critical for the sliding-filament mechanism of muscle contraction, with the H-zone representing the myosin-only region and the M-line serving as the central anchor point for thick filaments.
figures.fig-7-4: This is a two-panel schematic diagram illustrating the molecular architecture of thick and thin myofilaments（粗肌絲與細肌絲）within skeletal muscle sarcomeres（肌節）. Panel (a) depicts a thick filament（粗肌絲）composed of multiple myosin molecules bundled together, with a magnified inset showing the structural subunits of a single myosin molecule: a coiled-coil myosin tail（肌球蛋白尾部）and two globular myosin heads（肌球蛋白頭部）projecting outward, consistent with the bipolar arrangement that allows cross-bridge cycling. Panel (b) depicts a portion of a thin filament（細肌絲）showing three distinct protein components: globular actin（肌動蛋白）monomers arranged in a double-helical strand rendered in yellow-orange, rod-like tropomyosin（原肌球蛋白）molecules depicted as brown strands running longitudinally along the actin helix, and troponin（肌鈣蛋白）complexes shown as dark teal clusters positioned at regular intervals along the tropomyosin strand; critically, each myosin-binding site（肌球蛋白結合位點）on actin is explicitly labeled as being covered by tropomyosin in the resting state, illustrating the steric-blocking mechanism of muscle contraction regulation. The figure directly supports the surrounding textbook text's claim that the I-band predominantly contains actin protein, by showing the full molecular composition of the thin filament that occupies that band, and that the A-band contains myosin, by showing the thick filament's myosin tail and heads. The blocked myosin-binding sites on actin in panel (b) provide the structural basis for understanding why calcium-induced conformational changes in troponin and tropomyosin are required to initiate cross-bridge formation between myosin heads and actin during excitation-contraction coupling（興奮收縮耦聯）. No numerical scale bars, axes, or quantitative units are present in either panel.
figures.fig-7-5: This is an anatomical schematic diagram illustrating the motor unit, comprising a cross-sectional view of the spinal cord on the left, two α-motor neurons (運動神經元) with their axons extending rightward, and a group of skeletal muscle fibres (肌纖維) on the right. There are no numerical axes, units, or scale bars; the figure is purely diagrammatic and color-coded. Every labeled element is as follows: "Spinal cord" (脊髓) — shown in cross-section with butterfly-shaped grey matter (灰質) and surrounding white matter (白質), with two motor neuron cell bodies originating from the ventral horn; "Motor neurons" — two distinct axons, one rendered in green and one in purple, each arising from the spinal cord and coursing through a shared nerve bundle (shown in yellow/gold) before diverging toward separate populations of muscle fibres; "Neuromuscular junction" (神經肌肉接合處) — the terminal arborization of each axon onto the sarcolemma（肌纖維膜）of individual muscle fibres, depicted as branched green and purple terminal boutons (突觸小體) contacting the surfaces of the cylindrical muscle fibres; and "Muscle fibres (cells)" — multiple striated, cylindrical fibres shown in red, each receiving synaptic input at the neuromuscular junction from one of the two motor neurons. The figure directly illustrates the surrounding text's definition of a motor unit as a single α-motor neuron plus all the muscle fibres it innervates: the green motor neuron innervates one distinct subset of fibres and the purple motor neuron innervates a separate subset, visually demonstrating that one motor neuron can synapse with multiple fibres while each fibre receives input from only one motor neuron. The neuromuscular junction depicted here is the anatomical locus through which the action potential of the α-motor neuron is transduced into muscle fibre activation, providing the neural input described in Section 2.1.2.5 as prerequisite for muscle contraction and contextually linking to the upstream discussion of sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium release via excitation–contraction coupling (興奮–收縮耦聯).
figures.fig-7-12: This is a line plot (myogram) depicting the force of contraction (y-axis, unlabeled numeric scale) versus time in milliseconds (x-axis, ranging from 0 to approximately 60 msec, linear scale), illustrating the three sequential sub-phases of a single skeletal muscle twitch contraction（單收縮）following a stimulus applied at time = 0 ms (indicated by an upward arrow). The **latent period**（潛伏期）, rendered in blue, spans approximately 0–5 msec and represents the brief delay between the stimulus onset and the first detectable rise in contractile force, during which Ca²⁺ is released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum（肌漿網）and diffuses to the myofilaments to initiate cross-bridge cycling. The **contraction period**（收縮期）, rendered in red, spans approximately 5–28 msec, during which force rises in a sigmoidal trajectory to reach peak tension at roughly 28 msec. The **relaxation period**（舒張期）, rendered in green, spans approximately 28–55 msec, during which force declines back to baseline as Ca²⁺ is actively re-sequestered into the SR by SERCA pumps（肌漿網鈣離子ATP酶）and cross-bridge detachment predominates. The total twitch duration of ~55 msec aligns directly with the surrounding text's assertion that twitch contraction/relaxation lasts 20–200 msec—far exceeding the 1–2 msec duration of the single action potential（動作電位）that triggers it—and visually demonstrates the temporal mismatch between electrical excitation and mechanical response. The figure thus concretely illustrates why a latent period exists (the excitation–contraction coupling（興奮–收縮耦聯）delay for Ca²⁺ signaling), and why fibre-type-dependent differences in Ca²⁺ handling and myosin ATPase isoform kinetics produce variable twitch durations across the 20–200 msec range noted in the text.
figures.fig-7-11: This is a line plot (曲線圖) with fibre recruitment (纖維募集) on the y-axis and exercise intensity (運動強度) on the x-axis, both axes unlabeled with numerical values or units, indicating a schematic/conceptual representation rather than a quantitative graph. Three curves are plotted, each corresponding to a distinct muscle fibre type (肌纖維類型): Type I (slow-twitch oxidative, 慢縮氧化型, blue curve), Type IIa (fast-twitch oxidative-glycolytic, 快縮氧化醣解型, red curve), and Type IIx (fast-twitch glycolytic, 快縮醣解型, green curve). The Type I (blue) curve rises earliest from the origin, plateaus at a moderate fibre recruitment level, and flattens well before maximal exercise intensity, reflecting that slow-twitch motor units with small motor neurons (運動神經元) are recruited first and reach saturation at low-to-moderate intensities. The Type IIx (green) curve begins rising from the origin simultaneously with or slightly later than Type I but ascends more steeply and continues increasing to the highest fibre recruitment level across the full range of exercise intensities, ultimately surpassing all other fibre types; notably, its trajectory overlaps with and runs above the Type IIa curve throughout most of the intensity range. The Type IIa (red) curve begins its distinct separation from the green IIx curve at approximately mid-range exercise intensity and reaches a high but submaximal recruitment plateau, positioned between Type I and Type IIx, consistent with the intermediate motor neuron size of IIa motor units. This figure directly illustrates the size principle (大小原則) and orderly recruitment principle (有序募集原則) described in the surrounding text: Type I fibres with small motor neurons are recruited first at low exercise intensities, Type IIa fibres are added at moderate-to-high intensities, and Type IIx fibres with the largest motor neurons contribute progressively at the highest intensities, explaining glycogen depletion patterns observed by Gollnick et al. (1974) wherein Type I glycogen is preferentially depleted at low-to-moderate intensities and Type IIa/IIx glycogen at high intensities.
figures.fig-7-13: This is a line plot overlaid with inset schematic diagrams illustrating the length–tension relationship of skeletal muscle sarcomeres（肌節）. The x-axis represents resting sarcomere length as a percentage of optimum (range: ~40–175%), and the y-axis represents tension developed as a percentage of maximum (range: 0–100%), both on linear scales. The red curve is bell-shaped with a flat plateau at 100% tension spanning approximately 80–120% of optimum sarcomere length (highlighted in green as the "optimal length" zone), flanked by an "Understretched" region to the left (below ~80%) and an "Overstretched" region to the right (above ~120%), with tension falling to ~0% at both extremes (~55% and ~175% of optimum). Four inset diagrams depict sarcomere cross-sections at specific lengths: at 1.8 µm (understretched, ~60% of optimum) thin and thick filaments overlap excessively, reducing tension to ~83%; at 2.2 µm (optimal, ~100%) maximal actin–myosin（肌動蛋白–肌球蛋白）cross-bridge formation yields 100% tension; at 3.8 µm (overstretched, ~170%) thin filaments (actin) and thick filaments (myosin) are pulled apart with minimal overlap, reducing tension toward 0%; a fourth inset at ~120% shows near-optimal overlap. The figure directly illustrates the concept of summation and tetanus discussed in the surrounding text by demonstrating that the tension a muscle fibre can develop during fused tetanic contraction（融合強直收縮）is critically dependent on the initial (resting) sarcomere length, because cross-bridge（橫橋）cycling efficiency—and hence the additive force generated by repeated action potentials—is maximal only when thick and thin filament overlap is optimal (~2.2 µm). The precise sarcomere dimensions annotated (1.8 µm, 2.2 µm, 3.8 µm) correspond to known structural measurements of myosin thick filaments (~1.6 µm) and actin thin filaments (~1.0–1.1 µm per half-sarcomere), linking ultrastructural geometry to the macroscopic tension output quantified on the y-axis.
figures.fig-7-14: This is a dual-panel line plot (myogram and action potential traces) with the x-axis labeled "Time (msec)" (no numerical tick marks provided, arrow indicating rightward progression) and the y-axis labeled "Force of contraction" (arrow indicating upward progression, no absolute units given), divided by three vertical dashed lines into four labeled sections: (a) Single twitch, (b) Wave summation, (c) Unfused tetanus, and (d) Fused tetanus. The upper panel displays a red myogram（肌動圖）trace and the lower panel displays a blue action potential（動作電位）trace, with a horizontal dotted line separating the two panels. In section (a), a single action potential elicits one isolated twitch（單次抽搐）with a smooth rise-and-fall force curve that returns fully to baseline; in section (b), two closely spaced action potentials produce wave summation（波疊加）, where the second contraction begins before the first fully relaxes, generating a dashed line indicating the expected decay of the first twitch and a visibly higher peak force for the second; in section (c), a higher-frequency stimulus train produces unfused tetanus（不完全強直收縮）, characterized by a staircase-like progressive increase in peak force with partial relaxation between stimuli; and in section (d), a still higher frequency produces fused tetanus（完全強直收縮）, where individual twitches merge into a single sustained, smooth, maximal-force plateau that is substantially higher than any individual twitch before declining sharply when stimulation ceases. The figure directly supports the surrounding text's discussion of the force–velocity relationship by demonstrating that graded increases in stimulus frequency (rate coding) progressively increase the force of contraction, from a single twitch through summation to maximal tetanic tension, illustrating that skeletal muscle force output is modulated by motor neuron firing rate and that slow, sustained contractions (as in fused tetanus) achieve the greatest absolute tension — mechanistically analogous to the principle that slow concentric velocities permit maximal force production on the force–velocity curve. Key scientific terminology includes: motor unit（運動單位）recruitment, wave summation, treppe（階梯效應）, incomplete and complete tetanus, Ca²⁺ re-release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum（肌漿網）as the underlying ionic mechanism sustaining elevated force, and the myogram as the graphical record of mechanical contractile output.
figures.fig-7-15: This is a line plot depicting the force–velocity relationship (力–速度關係) in skeletal muscle contraction, with the y-axis labeled "Velocity of shortening" (縮短速度) and the x-axis labeled "Load on muscle" (肌肉負荷); neither axis carries explicit numerical units or scale markings, rendering this a qualitative, schematic representation. A single continuous green hyperbolic curve originates at a high intercept on the y-axis (representing maximum unloaded shortening velocity, V_max) and descends asymptotically toward the x-axis as load increases, approaching zero velocity at maximum isometric force (P_0), consistent with the Hill equation: (P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a)b, where P is load, v is shortening velocity, and a and b are constants with dimensions of force and velocity respectively. No secondary curves, annotations, data points, or labeled landmarks (such as V_max or P_0 intercepts) are explicitly marked on the graph beyond the axis labels. The hyperbolic decay directly illustrates the textbook claim that "the absolute force produced during contraction is greatest at slow, concentric speeds" and that "the speed of contraction is greatest at low workloads," visually encoding the inverse relationship between external load and concentric shortening velocity applicable to both slow-twitch (Type I) and fast-twitch (Type II) muscle fibres. This curve underpins the concept of power output (功率输出; force × velocity) optimization discussed in the surrounding text, demonstrating that peak power—the product of force and velocity—occurs at an intermediate load rather than at either extreme of the curve, which has direct implications for resistance training prescription in sport and exercise contexts.
figures.fig-7-10: This figure is a composite schematic diagram and electron micrograph series illustrating sarcomere shortening during muscle contraction across three states: (a) relaxed, (b) partially contracted, and (c) maximally contracted muscle. Each panel pairs a left-side schematic showing the molecular arrangement of thick filaments (myosin) and thin filaments (actin) with a right-side scanning electron micrograph (SEM) displaying the corresponding striation pattern. In the relaxed state (a), the sarcomere spans two Z-discs and contains clearly demarcated zones: the H-zone (containing only thick filaments at the center), the I-band (containing only thin filaments, appearing light blue), and the A-band (the entire length of thick filaments, appearing pink, which includes the region of overlap with actin). The M-line bisects the H-zone at the sarcomere center, and the SEM shows distinct I-bands flanking the darker A-band with a visible H-zone. In partial contraction (b), the H-zone narrows as thin filaments are pulled toward the M-line by myosin cross-bridge cycling, reducing the distance between Z-discs while the A-band width remains constant; the SEM reflects reduced spacing between Z-discs and a smaller H-zone. In maximal contraction (c), the H-zone nearly disappears as actin filaments from opposite sides of the sarcomere meet near the M-line, the I-bands shorten substantially, and the sarcomere length is minimized, with the SEM showing closely apposed Z-discs and minimal light banding. This figure directly illustrates the sliding filament mechanism described in the surrounding text, where myosin heads "walk" along actin filaments toward the Z-disc, pulling actin toward the M-line in an ATP- and Ca²⁺-dependent process, thereby shortening the sarcomere without changing the length of individual thick or thin filaments.
figures.fig-7-1: This is a hierarchical anatomical illustration depicting the multi-scale structural organization of skeletal muscle from gross anatomy down to the molecular level. The top-left inset shows the macroscopic attachment of muscle to bone via tendons, with the belly (fleshy contractile portion) positioned between tendons and a transverse plane indicated to orient the subsequent cross-sectional views. The main progression moves from left to right and top to bottom, beginning with the whole muscle encased in periosteum at the bone-tendon junction and epimysium (outermost connective tissue sheath) surrounding the belly of skeletal muscle, then magnifying to reveal fascicles (bundles of muscle fibers) wrapped by perimysium. Further magnification shows individual muscle fibers (cells) enveloped by endomysium, with each fiber containing multiple peripherally located nuclei, visible striations (alternating light and dark bands characteristic of sarcomeric organization), and the sarcolemma (plasma membrane) enclosing the sarcoplasm (cytoplasm). The illustration explicitly labels blood capillaries and somatic motor neurons within the endomysium, demonstrating the neuromuscular and vascular supply essential for muscle function. At the highest magnification, transverse sections through a fasciculus reveal the arrangement of myofibrils within each muscle fiber, with myofibrils composed of repeating sarcomeres containing myofilaments (thick and thin filaments of myosin and actin, respectively). This figure directly supports the surrounding text's discussion of muscle fiber structure, particularly the multinucleated nature, cylindrical shape extending the muscle's full length (from millimeters to 30 cm), fiber diameter range (10–120 μm), and the hierarchical wrapping by endomysium, perimysium, and epimysium connective tissue layers that organize fibers into functional fascicles and whole muscles.
figures.fig-7-7: This is a vertical flowchart schematic diagram depicting the hierarchical chain of neural and cellular events from voluntary motor planning in the brain to force generation in skeletal muscle. The diagram begins at the top with "Planning of willed movements," which bifurcates into two parallel descending pathways: "Motor cortical & other corticospinal outputs" and "Other supraspinal and propriospinal outputs," with a lateral arrow indicating cross-communication between these two systems. Both pathways converge onto a highlighted circular node labeled "α and γ motor neurons" (alpha and gamma motor neurons), which represents the final common pathway from the central nervous system. The flow continues linearly downward through "Motor axons," then to "Neuromuscular junction and sarcolemma" (the NMJ and muscle fiber membrane), followed by "Excitation–contraction coupling" (the process linking electrical depolarization to mechanical contraction), and terminates at "Cross-bridge force ATP hydrolysis" (the molecular mechanism of actin-myosin interaction powered by adenosine triphosphate breakdown). This schematic directly illustrates the surrounding text's assertion that voluntary muscle contractions originate as nerve impulses from the motor cortex traveling through the spinal cord and motor neurons to skeletal muscle fibers, providing a simplified top-down view of the complete command-to-contraction sequence that precedes the detailed action potential propagation mechanisms described in Section 2.2.1.
figures.fig-7-9: This is a cyclic schematic diagram illustrating the four-step muscle contraction-relaxation cycle at the molecular level, depicting the interaction between thick filaments (myosin, shown in orange/brown) and thin filaments (actin, shown in red), with myosin heads (shown as red globular structures) protruding from the myosin backbone. The cycle begins at step 1 (top left) where myosin heads hydrolyze ATP into ADP and inorganic phosphate (P), causing the heads to become reoriented and energized in a "cocked" position; step 2 (top right) shows the energized myosin heads binding to actin to form crossbridges, with ADP and P still attached; step 3 (bottom right) depicts the power stroke, where myosin crossbridges rotate toward the center of the sarcomere (the M-line), releasing ADP and pulling the actin filaments inward; and step 4 (bottom left) shows ATP binding to the myosin heads, causing the crossbridges to detach from actin and return to the starting configuration. Purple spheres in the key represent calcium ions (Ca²⁺), and the central text box states "Contraction cycle continues if ATP is available and Ca²⁺ level in the sarcoplasm is high," directly linking to the surrounding text's explanation that continual ATP supply and calcium availability are required for repeated cycling. The diagram visually demonstrates the sliding filament mechanism described in the text, showing how myosin "walks" along actin filaments toward the Z-disc, pulling actin toward the M-line with each power stroke, and how ATP binding causes myosin release to enable the next contraction cycle.

meta.note: fig-7-9 + fig-7-10 (cyclic sub-type) Sonnet 描述 pending 修修 verdict — 若 verdict 升 Opus，這 2 張要 rerun；其餘 13 張 spot-check 已通過子類別可直接用。Image > 5MB 已 PIL LANCZOS resize to longest-side 1600px.
